sentences.text: So then we remained.
And we-- we looked at each other.
And we said, what will be now, our future?
So in order to-- to be together, we married in the 6th of-- of February, 1948.
And we-- we started to-- to look up, once we discussed what to do with ourselves, or to join our family and-- and commit suicide, or to go on living and tell the story.
And my husband was a great optimist.
So he said, we will start from anew.
And-- and we started to look for work.
But you know that the Jews don't-- don't sit idly.
We-- we-- we couldn't go out, but we went out.
And despite of the-- of the antisemitism.
And there was arranged, a committee.
And in that time, in 1946, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we started to work before we-- we got married.
Because-- because we-- we waited to hear something.
We-- But in 1946, we started, even, with a committee, that it was a political key, you know, from all political parties.
And the Communist Party was the-- was the leading one.
And there were arranged all kind of organizations.
And-- and there were arranged-- you know, there were all kinds of [NON-ENGLISH] organizations.
There was the Bund.
And the Bund was given-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --to arrange the cultural life.
So they got, from the government, a house that we occupied.
And there were-- there was a concert hall with a stage.
And there was another hall that-- that we arranged a-- a kitchen for people who came from all over.
And from Russia came the refugees.
And from the camps, and from the concentration camps came.
And in '46, there was, accumulated, about 35,000 Jews.
Because we always knew that we'll have to run away.
So Szczecin was about 60 kilometers from Berlin.
And they knew that if run, they have to run to the-- to-- to the German borders.
So then when the DP camps were arranged, and then when the people were running, so until then, we have got to live.
And-- and not only bread alone.
We have got to-- to have spiritual-- spiritually live too.
So we arranged.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Every week, we met.
And we learned songs that we sang.
Yiddish songs.
Because whoever were in the-- in the concentration camps, they didn't sing Yiddish songs.
And Russians learned Russian songs.
So I was the leader, musically.
I learned with them every week, a song.
And the other week we repeated the song.
And whoever wanted to sing it solo, and we learned another song.
The Joint helped to arrange [NON-ENGLISH], it was called.
It means, trade places, where people at their trade, could find work.
The committee was arranged that whoever came and signed, they knew who he was, where from, what his trade was.
And that's why they could find, even, families, relationships, and the-- when the-- what the trades.
They knew how many people they have for that trade, for that trade.
And they arranged places, work places.
And that's how life started to-- to-- to-- to go on, until they have got to run again.
But we started to give concerts.
There came a Polish theater into the city.
And the-- the main artist was-- was Maria Malicka, who were from Poland.
They knew the name.
And she was on the level like Greta Garbo, that level artist, from Warsaw.
And my husband happened to work with Polish artists in Warsaw.
He was the pianist that everybody wanted, him to accompany.
So they arranged.
There was concerts or-- or a tour before the war.
And he was-- and Malicka was one of his colleagues.
So when we went the first time, to a Polish play, and we went backstage and she saw him, she couldn't believe, first of all.
She was shocked that he was with one hand.
And secondly, that-- they met.
And we became very, very good friends.
She used to come to us always.
And there was a Polish poet, [INAUDIBLE].
He was on the same level, as I told you, [? to-- ?] [? to ?] [? him. ?] And he wrote a beautiful poem in Polish by the name, [POLISH] is peace in Polish.
She recited.
And my husband gave a musical interlude-- interlude.
And wherever she went, she didn't go without him.
Then we arranged a concert too, in Poland.
And she was-- also, she recited.
I was singing.
We had a-- a violinist, [?
Dolgova. ?] And-- and my husband accompanied everybody.
And then he played solo.
So it was a beautiful ensemble.
And that's how we lived until the Jews started to-- to run away again.
They wanted to take us.
We didn't want to run.
I said, if I can't go legally somewhere else, I-- I won't leave.
I haven't got the-- the-- the-- the strength to-- to.
We will have to start from anew, wherever we will come.
And that-- that-- we remained.
And we had an half an hour in the Polish radio, where we gave-- a half an hour-- we had the journalist with us, Dr.
[INAUDIBLE] from Warsaw.
He wrote a-- a weekly journal in Polish, but about Jewish life in Szczecin.
So he read, always, a lecture.
He gave a lecture.
And I sang Jewish songs at the radio.
So I have to tell you an episode.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The director of the radio once called me.
And he said, Sara, you sing so beautiful, the Jewish song-- songs.
You-- you know-- you know, I'm sure that you know, also, Polish folk songs.
Maybe you will give us once, an audition.
I said, with pleasure.
They were so good to-- to us, and gave us weekly-- I said, yes.
I'll prepare a nice program of Polish songs.
And I'll sing it for you, gladly.
Well, with my husband.
In Poland, eight o'clock in the evening was suppertime.
They-- in Europe, they always had the main meal daytime, like here, lunch.
And suppertime they had like, a bite.
You know?
But eight o'clock, everybody was home.
So eight o'clock was the-- the performance.
And I picked nice Polish songs.
Every people has its own musical heritage.
And it was really very nice, and you know, very successful.
I showed you the picture also, that.
And we, as invalids, have got to go every half a year, or every year, to a doctor, to a military doctor.
He should overlook us.
I don't know why.
But that's it.
Because there, we-- we got a pension, as invalids, from the war.
So probably that.
And it was the next day of our audition.
It means the-- the singing in the radio.
I-- we came in.
And he said, Fershko?
Aren't you the lady that sang yesterday evening, in the radio?
I said, yes.
He said, it was wonderful.
It was a real happening.
So I said, thank you.
Then he said-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a Jewess sings so beautifully in Polish.
If you took a dagger, and give me it-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said, why shouldn't I?
I finished full Polish schools.
Polish is my-- is my second mother language.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Since then, I gave a vow to myself, that I'll never sing Polish.
And I didn't.
I sang in five, six, languages in my concerts, but never in Polish.
And even the-- the-- the director of the Polish theater-- theater, who was a Jew, wrote a beautiful romantic song in Polish.
And my husband composed the music.
And it's a beautiful song.
And I never sang it publicly.
This was my reaction until today.
I speak Polish when-- when the occasion came.
That-- that-- there are a lot of people still from Poland, speak Polish.
And this was an episode that it was-- that gave me also-- this is antisemitism.
It's in any form, you can find it.
He enjoyed my singing.
But he has got to give me-- so that's-- that's life.
And we remained in Poland.
And we couldn't-- the brother of my husband, from Argentina, send us a visa to come to Argentina.
And we couldn't take advantage on it.
When we came to Warsaw, to the Argentina consulate, they-- they looked at us like-- like we-- we-- we fell from the skies.
That it's the first time that we had somebody to have a visa straight to Argentina.
Because all the Jews have-- haven't been let in.
They have got to go all around.
The Nazis came to-- to Argentina freely.
But the Jews, not.
We couldn't because to Israel, they-- they let go some Jews from Poland.
But this was in 1950, when they started to-- to go back, not to let out the Jews.
And we couldn't get out because even the Jewish communists didn't let us out.
Because we were very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] valuable to them.
And we went to Warsaw.
We came to the-- to the-- to the bureau of-- to the travel-- not travel bureau, but the-- to the government, to ask what we can do.
With bayonets, they said-- they-- they-- they pushed us away.
But we found out that one general, he was the one who could say yes or no about immigration.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we found a way to come to find him.
And we have an audience with him.
And he said, Mr. and Mrs.
Fershko, you are such wonderful artists.
We'll give you a Steinway piano, what you deserve.
You have to live in the capital.
And-- and we should enjoy your artistry.
When he finished I said, General, every stone in Warsaw, is soaked in Jewish blood.
We can't live, anymore, in-- in Warsaw.
We live in Szczecin because it also-- it was a broken city, a demolished city after the war.
But we didn't know Szczecin before the war.
But we knew Warsaw before the war.
If you want, really, to help us, help us to go to Israel.
He said, Israel?
A country that's just born and starts from anew.
So I say, Israel starts from anew.
We have to start from anew.
Next day, all the doors that were closed, were open.
And it was please.
Please, in two hours we had the visa.
But I'm talking all about us.
And now I want to tell you, that in Szczecin, we met a wonderful lady, who went through a lot in her life during the war.
Her name was Elsa Frank.
She was the only daughter.
Her birth place was Danzig.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she had three brothers.
And her father, thank God, died in 1933, when Hitler came to power, and her mother.
They were well-to-do people.
And she married a doctor from Berlin, an-- Dr.
Frank.
And her brother was told a day before-- you see, they are different.
They are Nazis.
And Nazis also, and SS.
He was sitting with him on the same bench in the-- in the school.
And he came to the bureau of Elsa's brother.
And he said, Fritz, I saw your name, that tonight-- no, tomorrow night, they will come at 11 o'clock at night.
And they'll took-- take away your home and everything.
Take your family today and go away!
You see the difference?
He was a Nazi who gave him a tip.
And my girlfriend turned away when the war broke out.
You see?
And the same day, Elsa, with her husband, they were two brothers, and the mother.
Because one son was in Israel when he was 14 years old.
He couldn't attend, already, school in Germany.
So he was sent to Israel, by HaBonim.
So he lived in Israel.
And they left for Warsaw with the last train.
They couldn't take-- probably they took jewelry, or cash, what they had.
And they left the-- the-- the-- the help there with-- you can imagine how they-- they lived.
And they left for Warsaw.
And as he said, next day they came.
And they demolished the house.
And-- and from Warsaw, later, the mother called the help.
And she said, my goodness, good that you haven't been there.
They came and they demolished the house.
And they took away everything.
And that was that.
And they have been in Warsaw the 6th of September, '39, when-- when the Nazis came into Warsaw.
And they took all the men for work.
And whoever hid some of us, they brought him, and they beat him.
Elsa was at the balcony.
Her husband was downstairs in the line also, to go with her brother.
As a doctor, they brought somebody who fell and bleeded.
He wanted to give him help.
He was shot on the spot.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Elsa, they were five years married, without children but-- so she became a widow right away.
And then they struggled.
And-- and-- and Elsa remained alone.
And her family scattered somewheres.
They didn't know one from each other.
And she were-- she was beaten by the Germans.
And she knew-- didn't know another language but German.
But the Poles said, no she's Jewish.
And they-- they-- they beat her.
And she started to run.
So people said, come with us.
We'll-- we'll cross the border.
And we'll go to-- to the Soviets.
And there they-- they will-- she did.
She almost drowned.
And instead to come and started to live, she was caught, and went to prison.
From prison they sent her to-- to a camp.
And she went through-- not-- not less that she would be in-- in a German-- but she came out alive, sick.
And she came in '46, also to Szczecin.
And she came in, in that time, when all the Germans were-- were throwed out.
And-- and she didn't know Polish.
And she didn't know another language.
So she played the part of a mute, and didn't speak for two years, until we met accidentally.
With friends she came to a-- to a party that was made for us, for me and my husband, to thank us for everything that we did.
That I told you, we arranged a theater.
And we gave Jewish plays, like and I played Leah.
And-- and we really, we were well known in Szczecin, in the cultural world.
We brought in the Jewish culture back from-- from the war, after the war.
So there was-- and those friends were also in Jewish literature.
And they came to-- and they brought her for the first time.
And we got acquainted.
And she made an impression.
A very delicate face.
And she was so-- you know, shy.
And she didn't talk to-- to anybody because she talked German.
She-- and it came out that she lived half a block from me.
So we started to come to each other.
But she was sad.
And-- and she couldn't-- and once I came in, accidentally, I don't know why.
I went in to see her.
And I took her off a rope.
She wanted to commit suicide.
The second time, she opened all the gas.
She wanted to commit suicide.
Then I take her to my house.
She started to live with me.
And I said, I won't speak to-- from German into Yiddish, she couldn't change.
I said, but Polish, you hear all around you.
I'll speak to you, Polish.
You'll learn the language.
And my husband-- you know, we were stars.
So we-- we could arrange something.
We arranged, for her, work in a factory, where they-- where they made leather goods, gloves, leather pocketbooks, all kinds of leather goods.
And she-- she were told what to do.
And she did it.
So she started to get herself awareness as a-- as a person.
And she started to earn some money.
And she lived with us.
Whatever she earned, we gave in to, then-- then it was a bank, like [NON-ENGLISH] they called it.
And she was a part of our family.
And accidentally, she found a brother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One of the brothers, he was also in-- in Russia.
And he survived.
And he came to Szczecin, to see her.
And they met.
And he lived in-- in another town, had a wife, with a child.
And she went to visit him.
And it was very nice.
And she came back.
And-- and they-- they were in contact.
And it was very nice.
Then he suddenly, he was maybe in his 50's.
He got a heart attack and he passed away, in a minute.
And she got a stroke.
She couldn't go to the funeral.
She couldn't.
She was very sick.
She was with me.
The doctor used to come to the house.
Then she-- she got worse and worse.
She was taken to the hospital.
She was four months, in the hospital.
But they were taking very good care of her.
And she-- they-- they brought her out of this misery.
But she needed help.
So we came everyday.
And she needed to eat well.
So we brought everything up.
She could not forget that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She become well.
She came with us.
And when we left, we tried to take her too.
It was also-- it's-- it's happy though, that I can write a book about it, how we took her out of Poland.
But it-- it would be too much, once after the other.
But we went, together, to Israel.
And then she met her brother.
Her brother took her to her home, had a wife and one child.
And he had prepared a room for her.
And she should live with them, and then go to ulpan, and learn-- and study the language.
We went to a [NON-ENGLISH].
It was in a [NON-ENGLISH], near Tel Aviv.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she came to visit us.
And she saw-- [LAUGHS]-- in what circumstances we live there.
And without we had to-- to bring a little bit of water.
And-- and it was very inconvenient.
And when she saw how we live, she said to her brother, you see my dear, thanks to those people, I am alive.
I was able to come to see you.
You have a wonderful family.
And I am very thankful to you.
He was 10 years single-- younger.
And he didn't forget that she took care of him as a child.
So she said, I have to help, now, those people who helped me.
And she took her things.
And she came to the [NON-ENGLISH], to live with us.
And the Director came.
He was from Vilna, and brought another cart.
And there were three carts together.
And Elsa lived with us.
And then when we started to appear, Elsa went with us everywhere.
We were three people, one soul [? in ?] one pocket.
